title: Apply for a duty-free vehicle concession
description: Ask to bring in a vehicle without paying customs duty. Available for some individuals and businesses.
summary: Some people and businesses can bring in a vehicle without paying customs duty. This is called a duty-free concession. Returning nationals, people with disabilities, government employees, diplomats and certain authorised businesses are common categories.
There are two versions of the application: one for individuals and one for businesses. Both ask for the same vehicle details – registration, engine and chassis numbers, year and vehicle type.

Before you apply to the Barbados Licensing Authority, you usually need a letter of approval from the relevant ministry or agency. That letter is what tells the Licensing Authority you are eligible for a concession.

Once your concession is processed, the Licensing Authority will phone or email you to collect the document. The duty-free status only removes customs duty – other fees, like the licence fee, may still apply.
who_can_apply: an individual who meets the conditions for a duty-free concession (for example, returning national, government employee, person with a disability)
a business authorised by the Government to receive a duty-free concession

items: your name and ID number (or your business name)
your address, phone number and email
the registration number, engine number and chassis number of the vehicle

items.name: Approval letter from the relevant ministry or authority
Bill of sale for the vehicle

fees: There is no application fee. Duty-free status removes customs duties; other fees may still apply.
how_long: You will receive a phone call or email when your document is ready to collect.

options.heading: In person
content: Take the completed Request for Duty Free Concession letter (individual or business version) to the Barbados Licensing Authority.

contact.address:
The Pine, East–West Boulevard
St. Michael
BB11000

assumptions: We have merged the individual and business versions of the form onto one entry page.
‘A letter of approval from the relevant ministry or authority’ requirement is general practice. The form itself just asks for the vehicle details.
The categories of people who can apply (returning national, disability, government employee, etc.) are inferred from common Barbadian practice, not listed on the form.
